create table customer (name varchar(20), orderdate date, is_online char(3))
insert into customer values  ('Robert', '2022-01-01', 'yes'),('Robert', '2022-02-01', 'yes'),('Robert', '2022-03-01', 'yes'),
              ('Anna', '2022-01-01', 'yes'),('Anna', '2022-05-01', 'no'),('Anna', '2023-01-01', 'yes'),
              ('Steve', '2022-01-01', 'no'),('Steve', '2022-04-01', 'no'),('Steve', '2022-07-01', 'no'),
              ('Ross', '2023-01-01', 'yes'),('Ross', '2023-04-01', 'yes'),('Ross', '2023-07-01', 'no')
select name, orderdate, is_online from customer get the customer name who did not make any order in 2022

To fetch the customer names who did not make any order in 2022, you can use the following SQL query:

```sql
SELECT DISTINCT name
FROM customer
WHERE name NOT IN (
    SELECT name
    FROM customer
    WHERE YEAR(orderdate) = 2022
)
```

This query uses a subquery to identify the names of customers who made an order in 2022, and then retrieves the distinct names that are not present in that list.

SELECT mov_title, act_fname, act_lname, 
mov_year, role, gen_title, dir_fname, dir_lname, 
mov_dt_rel, rev_stars
FROM movie 
NATURAL JOIN movie_cast
NATURAL JOIN actor
NATURAL JOIN movie_genres
NATURAL JOIN genres
NATURAL JOIN movie_direction
NATURAL JOIN director
NATURAL JOIN rating
WHERE act_gender='F'; don't use natural join it won't work on sql

To retrieve the required data without using the NATURAL JOIN, you can use explicit JOIN statements and specify the join conditions. Here's the modified query:

```sql
SELECT movie.mov_title, actor.act_fname, actor.act_lname, 
       movie.mov_year, movie_cast.role, genres.gen_title, 
       director.dir_fname, director.dir_lname, movie.mov_dt_rel, rating.rev_stars
FROM movie 
JOIN movie_cast ON movie.mov_id = movie_cast.mov_id
JOIN actor ON movie_cast.act_id = actor.act_id
JOIN movie_genres ON movie.mov_id = movie_genres.mov_id
JOIN genres ON movie_genres.gen_id = genres.gen_id
JOIN movie_direction ON movie.mov_id = movie_direction.mov_id
JOIN director ON movie_direction.dir_id = director.dir_id
JOIN rating ON movie.mov_id = rating.mov_id
WHERE actor.act_gender = 'F';
```

This modified query uses explicit JOIN statements and specifies the join conditions using the ON keyword to join the appropriate tables. The WHERE clause filters the result to only include actors with a gender of 'F'.

SELECT mov_year,gen_title,count(gen_title), avg(rev_stars)
FROM movie 
NATURAL JOIN movie_genres 
NATURAL JOIN genres
NATURAL JOIN rating
WHERE gen_title='Mystery' 
GROUP BY mov_year,gen_title;  don’t use natural join it won’t work on sql

To retrieve the required data without using NATURAL JOIN, you can use explicit JOIN statements and specify the join conditions. Here's the modified query:

```sql
SELECT movie.mov_year, genres.gen_title, COUNT(genres.gen_title), AVG(rating.rev_stars)
FROM movie 
JOIN movie_genres ON movie.mov_id = movie_genres.mov_id
JOIN genres ON movie_genres.gen_id = genres.gen_id
JOIN rating ON movie.mov_id = rating.mov_id
WHERE genres.gen_title = 'Mystery'
GROUP BY movie.mov_year, genres.gen_title;
```

This modified query uses explicit JOIN statements and specifies the join conditions using the ON keyword to join the appropriate tables. The WHERE clause filters the result to only include movies with the genre title 'Mystery'. The GROUP BY clause groups the result by movie year and genre title.

SELECT mov_title, mov_year, rev_stars
FROM movie 
NATURAL JOIN movie_genres 
NATURAL JOIN genres 
NATURAL JOIN rating
WHERE gen_title = 'Mystery' AND rev_stars >= ALL (
SELECT rev_stars
FROM rating 
NATURAL JOIN movie_genres 
NATURAL JOIN genres
WHERE gen_title = 'Mystery');  don’t use natural join it won’t work on sql

To achieve the desired result without using NATURAL JOIN, you can use explicit JOIN statements and specify the join conditions. Here's the modified query:

```sql
SELECT movie.mov_title, movie.mov_year, rating.rev_stars
FROM movie
JOIN movie_genres ON movie.mov_id = movie_genres.mov_id
JOIN genres ON movie_genres.gen_id = genres.gen_id
JOIN rating ON movie.mov_id = rating.mov_id
WHERE genres.gen_title = 'Mystery' AND rating.rev_stars >= ALL (
    SELECT rating.rev_stars
    FROM rating
    JOIN movie_genres ON rating.mov_id = movie_genres.mov_id
    JOIN genres ON movie_genres.gen_id = genres.gen_id
    WHERE genres.gen_title = 'Mystery'
);
```

This modified query uses explicit JOIN statements and specifies the join conditions using the ON keyword to join the appropriate tables. The WHERE clause filters the result to only include movies with the genre title 'Mystery' and a rating that is equal to or greater than the highest rating given to any movie with the genre 'Mystery'.

I want to get this review stars except null values  on this movies  SELECT mov_title, MAX(rev_stars)
FROM movie
INNER JOIN rating USING(mov_id)
GROUP BY mov_title 
HAVING MAX(rev_stars)>0
ORDER BY mov_title;

To retrieve the movie title and maximum review stars (excluding null values) for each movie, you can use the following SQL query:

```sql
SELECT mov_title, MAX(rev_stars)
FROM movie
INNER JOIN rating ON movie.mov_id = rating.mov_id
WHERE rev_stars IS NOT NULL
GROUP BY mov_title
HAVING MAX(rev_stars) > 0
ORDER BY mov_title;
```

This query uses an INNER JOIN to combine the `movie` and `rating` tables based on the matching `mov_id` column. The WHERE clause filters out rows where `rev_stars` is null. The GROUP BY clause groups the result by `mov_title`. The HAVING clause filters out groups where the maximum `rev_stars` is zero or null. Finally, the result is ordered by `mov_title`.

From the following table, write a SQL query to find out which movies have received ratings. Return movie title, director first name, director last name and review stars.
don’t use natural join it won’t work on sql

Without using NATURAL JOIN, the SQL query to retrieve the movies that have received ratings, along with the movie title, director first name, director last name, and review stars would be as follows:

```sql
SELECT movie.mov_title, director.dir_fname, director.dir_lname, rating.rev_stars
FROM movie
JOIN movie_direction ON movie.mov_id = movie_direction.mov_id
JOIN director ON movie_direction.dir_id = director.dir_id
JOIN rating ON movie.mov_id = rating.mov_id;
```

This query uses explicit JOIN statements to combine the `movie`, `movie_direction`, `director`, and `rating` tables based on the matching IDs. The SELECT statement fetches the movie title, director first name, director last name, and review stars from the respective tables.
From the following table, write a SQL query to find out which movies have received ratings. Return movie title, director first name, director last name and review stars.

To find out which movies have received ratings and return the movie title, director first name, director last name, and review stars, you can use the following SQL query:

```sql
SELECT movie.mov_title, director.dir_fname, director.dir_lname, rating.rev_stars
FROM movie
INNER JOIN movie_direction ON movie.mov_id = movie_direction.mov_id
INNER JOIN director ON movie_direction.dir_id = director.dir_id
INNER JOIN rating ON movie.mov_id = rating.mov_id;
```

This query uses INNER JOIN statements to combine the `movie`, `movie_direction`, `director`, and `rating` tables based on their corresponding IDs. The SELECT statement retrieves the movie title, director first name, director last name, and review stars from the tables.

SELECT mov_title, dir_fname,dir_lname, rev_stars
FROM Movie
JOIN movie_direction USING(mov_id)
join director using (dir_id)
left join rating using(mov_id)
where rev_stars is not null;  From the following table, write a SQL query to find out which movies have received ratings. Return movie title, director first name, director last name and review stars. but i want to get this answer

Apologies for the mistake in my previous response. Here's the corrected SQL query:

```sql
SELECT movie.mov_title, director.dir_fname, director.dir_lname, rating.rev_stars
FROM movie
JOIN movie_direction ON movie.mov_id = movie_direction.mov_id
JOIN director ON movie_direction.dir_id = director.dir_id
LEFT JOIN rating ON movie.mov_id = rating.mov_id AND rating.rev_stars IS NOT NULL;
```

This query uses JOIN and LEFT JOIN statements to combine the `movie`, `movie_direction`, `director`, and `rating` tables based on their corresponding IDs. The LEFT JOIN ensures that all movies are included, regardless of whether they have a rating or not. The WHERE clause is removed, and instead, the condition `rating.rev_stars IS NOT NULL` is moved to the LEFT JOIN statement to filter out movies without ratings. That way, the result will only include movies that have received ratings, along with the movie title, director first name, director last name, and review stars.

To retrieve the movies that have received ratings, along with the movie title, director first name, director last name, and review stars, you can use the following SQL query:

```sql
SELECT movie.mov_title, director.dir_fname, director.dir_lname, rating.rev_stars
FROM movie
JOIN movie_direction ON movie.mov_id = movie_direction.mov_id
JOIN director ON movie_direction.dir_id = director.dir_id
JOIN rating ON movie.mov_id = rating.mov_id
ORDER BY movie.mov_title;
```

This query uses JOIN statements to combine the `movie`, `movie_direction`, `director`, and `rating` tables based on their corresponding IDs. The SELECT statement retrieves the movie title, director first name, director last name, and review stars from the tables. The result is ordered by movie title.

Please note that the query result will depend on the specific data in your table, and the format you provided is only an example of the expected result.
From the following table, write a SQL query to calculate the average movie length and count the number of movies in each genre. Return genre title, average time and number of movies for each genre.

To calculate the average movie length and count the number of movies in each genre, you can use the following SQL query:

```sql
SELECT genres.gen_title, AVG(movie.mov_length) AS average_duration, COUNT(*) AS movie_count
FROM movie
JOIN movie_genres ON movie.mov_id = movie_genres.mov_id
JOIN genres ON movie_genres.gen_id = genres.gen_id
GROUP BY genres.gen_title;
```

This query uses JOIN statements to combine the `movie`, `movie_genres`, and `genres` tables based on their corresponding IDs. The SELECT statement retrieves the genre title, calculates the average movie length using the AVG() function, and counts the number of movies using the COUNT() function. The result is grouped by genre title using the GROUP BY clause.
From the following table, write a SQL query to find movies with the shortest duration. Return movie title, movie year, director first name, last name, actor first name, last name and role.

To find movies with the shortest duration and return the movie title, movie year, director first name, director last name, actor first name, actor last name, and role, you can use the following SQL query:

```sql
SELECT movie.mov_title, movie.mov_year, director.dir_fname, director.dir_lname, actor.act_fname, actor.act_lname, movie_cast.role
FROM movie
JOIN movie_direction ON movie.mov_id = movie_direction.mov_id
JOIN director ON movie_direction.dir_id = director.dir_id
JOIN movie_cast ON movie.mov_id = movie_cast.mov_id
JOIN actor ON movie_cast.act_id = actor.act_id
WHERE movie.mov_length = (
    SELECT MIN(mov_length)
    FROM movie
)
```

This query uses JOIN statements to combine the `movie`, `movie_direction`, `director`, `movie_cast`, and `actor` tables based on their corresponding IDs. The WHERE clause specifies that only movies with the shortest duration (using a subquery to find the minimum duration) should be included in the result. The SELECT statement retrieves the movie title, movie year, director first name, director last name, actor first name, actor last name, and role for those movies.

SELECT mov_title, mov_year, dir_fname, dir_lname, 
       act_fname, act_lname, role 
FROM  movie
NATURAL JOIN movie_direction
NATURAL JOIN movie_cast
NATURAL JOIN director
NATURAL JOIN actor
WHERE mov_time=(SELECT MIN(mov_time) FROM movie);

The SQL query to retrieve movies with the shortest duration, along with the movie title, movie year, director first name, director last name, actor first name, actor last name, and role can be written as follows:

```sql
SELECT movie.mov_title, movie.mov_year, director.dir_fname, director.dir_lname, actor.act_fname, actor.act_lname, movie_cast.role
FROM movie
NATURAL JOIN movie_direction
NATURAL JOIN movie_cast
NATURAL JOIN director
NATURAL JOIN actor
WHERE movie.mov_length = (SELECT MIN(mov_length) FROM movie);
```

This query uses NATURAL JOIN to combine the `movie`, `movie_direction`, `movie_cast`, `director`, and `actor` tables based on their common column names. The WHERE clause filters the result to only include movies with the shortest duration, as determined by the subquery `SELECT MIN(mov_length) FROM movie`. The SELECT statement fetches the movie title, movie year, director first name, director last name, actor first name, actor last name, and role from the respective tables.

SELECT mov_title, mov_year, rev_stars
FROM movie 
NATURAL JOIN movie_genres 
NATURAL JOIN genres 
NATURAL JOIN rating
WHERE gen_title = 'Mystery' AND rev_stars >= ALL (
SELECT rev_stars
FROM rating 
NATURAL JOIN movie_genres 
NATURAL JOIN genres
WHERE gen_title = 'Mystery');   don’t use natural join it won’t work on sql

To achieve the same result without using NATURAL JOIN, you can use explicit JOIN statements and specify the join conditions. Here's the modified SQL query:

```sql
SELECT movie.mov_title, movie.mov_year, rating.rev_stars
FROM movie
JOIN movie_genres ON movie.mov_id = movie_genres.mov_id
JOIN genres ON movie_genres.gen_id = genres.gen_id
JOIN rating ON movie.mov_id = rating.mov_id
WHERE genres.gen_title = 'Mystery' AND rating.rev_stars >= ALL (
    SELECT rating.rev_stars
    FROM rating
    JOIN movie_genres ON rating.mov_id = movie_genres.mov_id
    JOIN genres ON movie_genres.gen_id = genres.gen_id
    WHERE genres.gen_title = 'Mystery'
);
```

This modified query uses explicit JOIN statements and specifies the join conditions using the ON keyword to join the appropriate tables. The WHERE clause filters the result to only include movies with the genre title 'Mystery' and a review star rating that is greater than or equal to the highest review star rating for movies with the same genre 'Mystery'. The SELECT statement retrieves the movie title, movie year, and review stars from the respective tables.
